claims.text: The paper "A Survey on Evaluation of LLM-based Agents" defines its scope as a comprehensive survey of evaluation methods for LLM-based agents, covering five perspectives: (1) core LLM capabilities for agentic workflows, (2) application-specific benchmarks such as web and SWE agents, (3) evaluation of generalist agents, (4) analysis of core benchmark dimensions, and (5) evaluation frameworks and tools for agent developers. It frames the field as a paradigm shift from static text-to-text LLMs to autonomous agents that plan, reason, and use tools in dynamic environments.
claims.verification_reason: Abstract and introduction explicitly enumerate the five perspectives, the paradigm shift framing, and planning/reasoning/tool use capabilities.

claims.text: The paper "Evaluating LLM-based Agents for Multi-Turn Conversations: A Survey" defines its scope as the evaluation of LLM-based agents specifically in multi-turn conversational settings, covering task-oriented and open-domain dialogue systems. It situates these systems historically from ELIZA through statistical and Transformer-based models to modern LLM-powered agents, and emphasizes the unique challenges of automated, reproducible, human-aligned evaluation for context-aware multi-turn interactions such as customer service and personal assistants.
claims.verification_reason: Keywords confirm task-oriented/open-domain dialogue; introduction confirms historical trajectory and emphasis on automated, reproducible, human-aligned evaluation for customer service/personal assistants.

claims.text: "A Survey on Evaluation of LLM-based Agents" analyzes agent benchmarks along shared orthogonal core dimensions: data curation, environment, interface, metric, and safety. Within safety and robustness, it highlights metrics such as pass^k (fraction of tasks successful across all k independent runs) and the need for "guardrail" metrics that penalize agents achieving task success via non-compliant actions like policy violations. The taxonomy is structural and cross-cutting rather than organized around what/how to evaluate.
claims.verification_reason: Section 5 explicitly defines the five orthogonal dimensions, defines pass^k, and describes guardrail metrics penalizing non-compliant actions.

claims.text: "Evaluating LLM-based Agents for Multi-Turn Conversations: A Survey" develops two interrelated taxonomy systems. The "what to evaluate" taxonomy identifies key components: task completion, response quality, user experience, memory and context retention, and planning and tool integration. The "how to evaluate" taxonomy categorizes methodologies into annotation-based evaluations, automated metrics (including BLEU, ROUGE, and BERTScore), hybrid strategies combining human and quantitative assessments, and self-judging methods utilizing LLMs.
claims.verification_reason: Abstract and conclusion explicitly enumerate both taxonomies with the listed components and methods, including BLEU, ROUGE, BERTScore and self-judging LLM methods.

claims.text: "A Survey on Evaluation of LLM-based Agents" surveys benchmarks across multiple categories: fundamental capability benchmarks (planning, tool use, self-reflection, memory); application-specific benchmarks for web agents, software engineering agents, scientific agents, and conversational agents; and generalist agent benchmarks such as Gaia, Gaia2, AgentBench, and the HAL (Holistic Agent Leaderboard). The survey observes a shift from application-specific toward general-purpose agents that integrate planning, reasoning, tool use, web interaction, file handling, and code execution.
claims.verification_reason: Introduction and Section 4 list fundamental capabilities, application-specific domains, generalist benchmarks (Gaia, Gaia2, AgentBench, HAL), and the shift toward general-purpose agents with integrated capabilities.

claims.text: "Evaluating LLM-based Agents for Multi-Turn Conversations: A Survey" surveys holistic multi-turn dialogue benchmarks organized into categories such as dialogue modeling (HUMOD, BotChat, AgentBench), and tool and action integration (API-Bank, MMDU, MT-Mind2Web, MTU-Bench, Factcheck-Bench). claims.text: "A Survey on Evaluation of LLM-based Agents" covers both end-to-end and component-level evaluation. items.span_text: LLM-based agents represent a paradigm shift in AI, enabling autonomous systems to plan, reason, and use tools while interacting with dynamic environments. This paper provides the first comprehensive survey of evaluation methods for these increasingly capable agents. We analyze the field of agent evaluation across five perspectives: (1) Core LLM capabilities needed for agentic workflows, like planning, and tool use; (2) Application-specific benchmarks such as web and SWE agents; (3) Evaluation of generalist agents; (4) Analysis of agent benchmarks� core dimensions; and (5) Evaluation frameworks and tools for agent developers. Our analysis reveals current trends, including a shift toward more realistic, challenging evaluations with continuously updated benchmarks. We also identify critical gaps that future research must address�particularly in assessing cost-efficiency, safety, and robustness, and in developing fine-grained, scalable evaluation methods <sup>1</sup>.
items.title: A Survey on Evaluation of LLM-based Agents

items.span_text: We begin by discussing the evaluation of fundamental LLM-based agent capabilities (�2). These include planning, tool use, self-reflection, and memory. We then review benchmarks and evaluation strategies for prominent types of agentic applications: web agents, software engineering agents, scientific agents, and conversational agents (�3). We continue to describe benchmarks and leaderboards for evaluating generalist agents (�4). Consequently, we define and analyze core dimensions of agent benchmarks (�5). �6 reviews current evaluation frameworks for agent developers. These frameworks integrate with the agent�s development environment, and support its evaluation throughout the entire development cycle. We conclude with a discussion (�7) of current trends and future research directions in agent evaluation. Figure 1 offers a visual summary of the survey�s structure.

Over time, model performance on Gaia improved, saturating the easier portion of the benchmark and emphasizing the need for an updated version. Andrews et al. items.span_text: Similar to the evolution of LLMs from task-specific into general-purpose models, agents are also transitioning from application-specific toward generalpurpose agents (Bandel et al., 2026b). Such tasks require integrated capabilities, from planning, reasoning, and tool use to web interaction, file handling, code execution, and more. This shift has led to the development of two complementary approaches to generalist agent evaluation.

items.section: 2.1 Evaluating the End-to-end Experience of LLM-based Agents For Multi-Turn Conversation
items.span_text: It is natural to prioritize evaluating the end-to-end experience in the first place, as this holistic approach directly captures real-world performance before delving into granular metrics. Multi-turn dialogues inherently difer from isolated single-turn queries in that they often encompass multiple sequential or interleaved tasks within a single conversation. This complexity necessitates evaluating an agent�s performance at the dialogue level, beyond individua exchanges. Specifically, we must assess not only whether each task is completed but also how well tasks are executed. items.span_text: The second approach for generalist agent evaluation relies on unifying several task-specific benchmarks into one. AgentBench (Liu et al., 2023) introduces interactive environments spanning OS operations, databases, games, and household tasks�highlighting core skills like flexibility and tool-based problem solving. HAL (Holistic Agent Leaderboard; Stroebl et al., 2025) provides a unified platform for benchmarks across domains, including coding and web. Yet, this approach does not support the same agent harness evaluation across any benchmark environment. Harbor and Exgentic aim to solve that by providing frameworks with a unified protocol for general agent assessment (Harbor Framework Team, 2026; Bandel et al., 2026a). These represent the first steps toward holistic, standardized, cross-environment agent evaluation (Lacoste et al., 2026).

items.section: 2.2 Evaluating The Action and Tool-use Components of LLM-based Agents for Multi-turn Conversation
items.span_text: 2.2.2 Multi-step Tool Selection and Reasoning . Multi-step tool use requires the agent�s "chain of thought" in action. I is not enough to select the right tool once; the agent must demonstrate contextual awareness to know when tools are needed, sequential logic to order them correctly across turns, and domain resilience to maintain this logic in specialized fields like math or coding. MetaTool [85] evaluates LLMs� tool awareness and decision-making using the ToolE dataset, which tests single- and multi-tool scenarios. It highlights challenges in tool selection, revealing gaps in LLM capabilities Wang et al. [225] introduced MTU-Bench to assess multi-tool usage and multi-step interactions across scenarios, including single- and multi-turn tasks, enhancing action execution in conversations. Nijkamp et al. [157] proposed a program synthesis benchmark focusing on breaking down complex programming tasks, while Wang et al. [226] developed a Python-based benchmark for evaluating tool-use in responses. Mathematical reasoning benchmarks were introduced by Kurtic et al. [102] and Sun et al. [207], focusing on dialogue contexts. Guo et al. [68] designed a benchmark for assessing multi-turn tool-use in Chinese societal applications, and Zhuang et al. [275] emphasized open-ended tool usage in question answering. Lastly, Huang et al. [81] proposed a comprehensive benchmark to evaluate the full tool-use process, including planning and creation in complex tasks. These studies suggest that a unified evaluation framework for multi-step tool selection and reasoning should assess invocation precision, operational eficiency, and error recovery. It should also evaluate tool awareness, planning capability, execution accuracy, and multi-turn coherence.
items.title: Evaluating LLM-based Agents for Multi-Turn Conversations: A Survey

items.span_text: � Holistic Evaluation Benchmarks and Datasets: As LLMs advanced, the field shifted toward more comprehensive benchmarks that incorporate human judgments, advanced automatic metrics such as BERTScore [97, 258], and task-specific evaluations to reflect the increasingly complex, context-rich nature of multi-turn interactions [10, 46]. The complexity of multi-turn dialogues led to the creation of specialized datasets and benchmarks tha focus on realism, domain knowledge, and long-horizon reasoning: (1) Dialogue Modeling: HUMOD [145] for human-like dialogue modeling; BotChat [50] for open-ended chat scenarios; AgentBench [133] for agent-centric tasks. (2) Tool and Action Integration: API-Bank [114] and MMDU [135] assess real-world tool usage, while MT-Mind2Web [46], MTU-Bench [225], and Factcheck-Bench [228] provide structured evaluations of multi-turn reasoning, user intent, and factual veracity.

items.span_text: Inclusion and Exclusion Criteria We defined strict criteria to maintain the survey�s focus and quality. Papers were included if they introduced a novel benchmark, an evaluation framework, or a significant methodological contribution to the assessment of LLM-based agents. Conversely, we excluded works that: 1. Proposed new agent architectures without a distinct contribution to evaluation methodology. 2. Focused solely on traditional LLM evaluation (e.g., reasoning on static datasets), lacking dynamic, agentic, or interactive components. While acceptance to top-tier conferences was utilized as a primary indicator of quality, we also included high-impact preprints to ensure the survey reflects the most recent advancements in this fast-paced field.

items.span_text: Multi-turn conversational agents powered by large language models (LLMs) mark a significant leap in conversational AI [74], ofering complex, context-aware interactions across multiple turns�essential for applications like customer service and personal assistants [38, 235]. Evaluating these systems poses unique challenges: methods must be automated, reproducible, and aligned with human judgments to ensure real-world reliability [247].
items.title: Evaluating LLM-based Agents for Multi-Turn Conversations: A Survey

items.section: Evaluating LLM-based Agents for Multi-Turn Conversations: A Survey
items.span_text: This survey examines evaluation methods for large language model (LLM)-based agents in multi-turn conversational settings. Using a PRISMA-inspired framework, we systematically reviewed nearly 250 scholarly sources, capturing the state of the art from various venues of publication, and establishing a solid foundation for our analysis. Our study ofers a structured approach by developing two interrelated taxonomy systems: one that defines what to evaluate and another that explains how to evaluate. The first taxonomy identifies key components of LLM-based agents for multi-turn conversations and their evaluation dimensions, including task completion, response quality, user experience, memory and context retention, as well as planning and tool integration. These components ensure that the performance of conversational agents is assessed in a holistic and meaningful manner. The second taxonomy system focuses on the evaluation methodologies. It categorizes approaches into annotation-based evaluations, automated metrics, hybrid strategies that combine human assessments with quantitative measures, and self-judging methods utilizing LLMs. This framework not only captures traditional metrics derived from language understanding, such as BLEU and ROUGE scores, but also incorporates advanced techniques that reflect the dynamic, interactive nature of multi-turn dialogues. Together, these frameworks summarize the current status quo, expose limitations in traditional practices, and provide a structured blueprint for improvement. Based on the summarization of existing studies, we identify several challenges and propose future directions, including the development of scalable, real-time evaluation pipelines, enhanced privacy-preserving mechanisms, and robust metrics that capture dynamic multi-turn interactions. Our contributions bridge historical insights with modern practices, paving the way for next-generation, reliably evaluated conversational AI systems and ofering a comprehensive guide for researchers and practitioners
items.title: Evaluating LLM-based Agents for Multi-Turn Conversations: A Survey

items.span_text: The shift from static models to adaptive, interactive agents calls for a new paradigm for evaluating LLM-based agents. Such evaluation must go beyond measuring LLM textual outputs to assess an agent�s capacity for sequential decision-making and operation within dynamic environments. It requires benchmarks that can assess the agent�s ability to accomplish user tasks via a sequence of actions and interactions. Moreover, benchmarks must co-evolve with agent capabilities, accommodating new classes of tasks and domains.
items.title: A Survey on Evaluation of LLM-based Agents

items.span_text: Safety and Robustness Metrics While most benchmarks prioritize capability, safety, and robustness are critical for enterprise adoption. Robustness is often quantified via pass^k, the fraction of tasks where the agent succeeds across all k independent runs. Beyond robustness, enterprise agents must adhere to strict policies, such as data privacy and access control, which are rarely tested in standard benchmarks. For instance, SWE-Lancer does not inherently penalize risky behaviors unless they interfere with replicating the target behavior. Future benchmarks must integrate "guardrail" metrics, penalizing agents that achieve task success via non-compliant actions (e.g., deleting production databases).
items.title: A Survey on Evaluation of LLM-based Agents

items.span_text: Realistic and Challenging Evaluation. While early evaluations often employed simplified, static environments, there is a clear shift toward more realistic and complex benchmarks. Web agents evolved from basic simulations to dynamic, realworld settings such as WebArena. In software engineering, benchmarks like SWE-bench utilize real GitHub issues, moving beyond synthetic coding tasks. Furthermore, there is increasing interest in long-horizon tasks typically performed by highly trained human experts, pushing evaluation closer to real-world professional workflows. This change reflects the growing need to assess agents� ability to derive practical value in realistic settings, to stresstest their limitations, and guide their progress.
items.title: A Survey on Evaluation of LLM-based Agents

items.span_text: Historically, multi-turn dialogue dates back to ELIZA [232], developed in the 1960s, which maintained simple states through pattern matching rather than complex context modeling. Subsequent progress incorporated finite-state machines, frame-based approaches, and statistical methods, culminating in the application of hidden Markov models (HMMs) [178] for spoken language understanding. Deep neural networks and Transformer architectures [217] then revolutionized context modeling, leveraging larger datasets and advanced training methods (including supervised and reinforcement learning) to handle more complex dialogues. In nowadays, multi-turn conversational systems often build on large-scale pre-trained language models, or namely Large Language Models (LLMs) [160], enabling advanced reasoning, continuity, and context handling over extended interactions.
items.title: Evaluating LLM-based Agents for Multi-Turn Conversations: A Survey

items.span_text: This paper provides a comprehensive overview of evaluation methods for LLM-based agents in multi-turn conversations. By systematically examining the evaluation goals�such as dialogue coherence, efective tool use, and memory retention�and the diverse methodologies, including annotation-based assessments and automated metrics, the study ofers a detailed synthesis of the progression from rule-based systems to transformer-based agents. This work lays a solid foundation for future explorations in the domain of conversational AI by addressing both theoretical and practical aspects. The core contribution of this paper lies in developing a structured taxonomy that clarifies critical dimensions of multi-turn conversational performance. This framework not only defines what elements should be evaluated but also establishes the methodological steps necessary to assess these components reliably. By synthesizing insights from nearly 200 scholarly sources, the study bridges the gap between existing evaluation methods and the emerging complexities inherent in modern LLM-based agents.
items.title: Evaluating LLM-based Agents for Multi-Turn Conversations: A Survey

items.span_text: Figure 1 illustrates an agent-based conversational system. Here, the user plans a trip to Europe; the agent suggests destinations, acknowledges a selection (Paris), confirms dates, and provides accommodation options. The agent�s planning process [84] breaks the request into actionable steps, narrowing the destination, confirming dates, and searching for relevant hotels. Tool-use [57, 161, 174], such as invoking search\_web for internet queries, yields structured data integrated into the final response. Two types of memory [24, 130]�conversation and turn memory�preserve dialogue context [259, 261]. The LLM interprets queries, formats responses, and manages complex language tasks [155], ensuring coherently adapted answers based on user inputs.
items.title: Evaluating LLM-based Agents for Multi-Turn Conversations: A Survey

items.span_text: LLMs have recently made remarkable progress, tackling a wide range of challenging tasks. Yet, LLMs are static, having fixed knowledge, and confined to text-to-text interaction. LLM-based agents address those gaps by building on LLMs as a backbone, integrating them into multi-step workflows and equipping them with external tools (Wang et al., 2024a). Hence, LLM agents can perform computations, retrieve up-to-date information, and interact with their environment. Crucially, they can autonomously plan, execute, and adapt complex strategies in real-world settings. This agency enables them to tackle problems once beyond the reach of AI, unlocking innovative applications across diverse domains.
items.title: A Survey on Evaluation of LLM-based Agents

items.span_text: The preceding sections reviewed benchmarks organized by agent type and application domain. Here, we take a broader perspective and analyze benchmarks along shared orthogonal dimensions: data curation, environment, interface, metric, and safety. This analysis reveals common structural patterns and gaps. In Table 1, we compare representative benchmarks based on these dimensions.
items.title: A Survey on Evaluation of LLM-based Agents

items.span_text: Advancing Granular Evaluation. Many current benchmarks rely on coarse-grained, end-to-end success metrics that, while useful for gauging overall performance, fall short in diagnosing specific agent failures. This lack of granularity obscures insights into intermediate decision processes such as tool selection and reasoning quality. Addressing this limitation calls for the development of standardized, fine-grained evaluation metrics targeting the trajectory of an agent�s task execution. Future work should explore detailed, step-by-step assessments to provide richer feedback and guide targeted improvements.
items.title: A Survey on Evaluation of LLM-based Agents

items.span_text: Scientific agents automate core research tasks by integrating domain knowledge and scientific tools. Their evaluation has progressed from basic reasoning benchmarks to comprehensive frameworks assessing diverse scientific research capabilities. Early efforts focused on knowledge recall and reasoning (Clark et al., 2018; Lu et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2022a), and literature understanding (Dasigi et al., 2021; Lee et al., 2023; DeYoung et al., 2021). More recent benchmarks like SciRiff (Wadden et al., 2024) broaden the scope to instructionfollowing across scientific domains.
items.title: A Survey on Evaluation of LLM-based Agents

claim_verdicts.reason: Abstract and introduction explicitly enumerate the five perspectives, the paradigm shift framing, and planning/reasoning/tool use capabilities.
claim_verdicts.verified_text: The paper "A Survey on Evaluation of LLM-based Agents" defines its scope as a comprehensive survey of evaluation methods for LLM-based agents, covering five perspectives: (1) core LLM capabilities for agentic workflows, (2) application-specific benchmarks such as web and SWE agents, (3) evaluation of generalist agents, (4) analysis of core benchmark dimensions, and (5) evaluation frameworks and tools for agent developers. It frames the field as a paradigm shift from static text-to-text LLMs to autonomous agents that plan, reason, and use tools in dynamic environments.

field_values.726cfbbd5683cdfabbf4c24e2bf8f0b2_scope: Evaluation methods for LLM-based agents in multi-turn conversational settings, spanning task-oriented and open-domain dialogue systems, historical context from ELIZA to LLM-based agents, and emphasis on automated, reproducible, human-aligned evaluation

claim_verdicts.reason: Keywords confirm task-oriented/open-domain dialogue; introduction confirms historical trajectory and emphasis on automated, reproducible, human-aligned evaluation for customer service/personal assistants.
claim_verdicts.verified_text: The paper "Evaluating LLM-based Agents for Multi-Turn Conversations: A Survey" defines its scope as the evaluation of LLM-based agents specifically in multi-turn conversational settings, covering task-oriented and open-domain dialogue systems. It situates these systems historically from ELIZA through statistical and Transformer-based models to modern LLM-powered agents, and emphasizes the unique challenges of automated, reproducible, human-aligned evaluation for context-aware multi-turn interactions such as customer service and personal assistants.

field_values.8b9e2f64d565137ca85ae75029e77fff_taxonom: Orthogonal core benchmark dimensions: data curation, environment, interface, metric, and safety (including pass^k robustness and guardrail metrics penalizing non-compliant task success)

claim_verdicts.reason: Section 5 explicitly defines the five orthogonal dimensions, defines pass^k, and describes guardrail metrics penalizing non-compliant actions.
claim_verdicts.verified_text: "A Survey on Evaluation of LLM-based Agents" analyzes agent benchmarks along shared orthogonal core dimensions: data curation, environment, interface, metric, and safety. Within safety and robustness, it highlights metrics such as pass^k (fraction of tasks successful across all k independent runs) and the need for "guardrail" metrics that penalize agents achieving task success via non-compliant actions like policy violations. The taxonomy is structural and cross-cutting rather than organized around what/how to evaluate.

field_values.726cfbbd5683cdfabbf4c24e2bf8f0b2_taxonom: Two interrelated taxonomies: (what) task completion, response quality, user experience, memory/context retention, planning and tool integration; (how) annotation-based, automated metrics (BLEU/ROUGE/BERTScore), hybrid human+quantitative, and LLM self-judging methods

claim_verdicts.reason: Abstract and conclusion explicitly enumerate both taxonomies with the listed components and methods, including BLEU, ROUGE, BERTScore and self-judging LLM methods.
claim_verdicts.verified_text: "Evaluating LLM-based Agents for Multi-Turn Conversations: A Survey" develops two interrelated taxonomy systems. The "what to evaluate" taxonomy identifies key components: task completion, response quality, user experience, memory and context retention, and planning and tool integration. The "how to evaluate" taxonomy categorizes methodologies into annotation-based evaluations, automated metrics (including BLEU, ROUGE, and BERTScore), hybrid strategies combining human and quantitative assessments, and self-judging methods utilizing LLMs.

field_values.8b9e2f64d565137ca85ae75029e77fff_benchma: Categories include fundamental capability benchmarks (planning, tool use, self-reflection, memory); application-specific (web, SWE, scientific, conversational); generalist benchmarks (Gaia, Gaia2, AgentBench, HAL); with a trend from application-specific toward general-purpose agents

claim_verdicts.reason: Introduction and Section 4 list fundamental capabilities, application-specific domains, generalist benchmarks (Gaia, Gaia2, AgentBench, HAL), and the shift toward general-purpose agents with integrated capabilities.
claim_verdicts.verified_text: "A Survey on Evaluation of LLM-based Agents" surveys benchmarks across multiple categories: fundamental capability benchmarks (planning, tool use, self-reflection, memory); application-specific benchmarks for web agents, software engineering agents, scientific agents, and conversational agents; and generalist agent benchmarks such as Gaia, Gaia2, AgentBench, and the HAL (Holistic Agent Leaderboard). The survey observes a shift from application-specific toward general-purpose agents that integrate planning, reasoning, tool use, web interaction, file handling, and code execution.

field_values.726cfbbd5683cdfabbf4c24e2bf8f0b2_benchma: Categories include dialogue modeling benchmarks (HUMOD, BotChat, AgentBench) and tool/action integration benchmarks (API-Bank, MMDU, MT-Mind2Web, MTU-Bench, Factcheck-Bench), emphasizing realism, long-horizon reasoning, multi-turn tool usage, and factual veracity

claim_verdicts.reason: Section 4.1 explicitly lists the dialogue modeling and tool/action integration benchmarks with stated emphasis on realism, domain knowledge, long-horizon reasoning, multi-turn tool usage, user intent, and factual veracity.
claim_verdicts.verified_text: "Evaluating LLM-based Agents for Multi-Turn Conversations: A Survey" surveys holistic multi-turn dialogue benchmarks organized into categories such as dialogue modeling (HUMOD, BotChat, AgentBench), and tool and action integration (API-Bank, MMDU, MT-Mind2Web, MTU-Bench, Factcheck-Bench). The survey emphasizes benchmarks that assess realism, domain knowledge, long-horizon reasoning, multi-turn tool usage, user intent understanding, and factual veracity in conversational contexts.

field_values.8b9e2f64d565137ca85ae75029e77fff_evaluat: Both end-to-end benchmarks (e.g., SWE-bench, WebArena) and component-level evaluation by capability (planning, tool use, self-reflection, memory); identifies a gap toward more granular, trajectory-level metrics as a future direction

claim_verdicts.reason: Introduction organizes by capabilities (planning, tool use, self-reflection, memory) and application domains; Section 7.2 explicitly calls for advancing granular evaluation beyond coarse-grained end-to-end success metrics with fine-grained trajectory assessment.
claim_verdicts.verified_text: "A Survey on Evaluation of LLM-based Agents" covers both end-to-end and component-level evaluation. It organizes the field by agent capabilities (planning, tool use, self-reflection, memory) and by application domains, and explicitly identifies as a future direction the need for "advancing granular evaluation" beyond coarse-grained end-to-end success metrics, calling for standardized, fine-grained, step-by-step assessment of agent trajectories such as tool selection and reasoning quality.

field_values.726cfbbd5683cdfabbf4c24e2bf8f0b2_evaluat: Prioritizes end-to-end dialogue-level evaluation that captures real-world multi-turn performance, while also providing component-level evaluation of action and tool-use (e.g., MetaTool/ToolE, MTU-Bench, multi-step reasoning and math benchmarks in dialogue contexts)

claim_verdicts.reason: Section 2.1 explicitly prioritizes end-to-end dialogue-level evaluation; Section 2.2 explicitly covers multi-step tool selection (MetaTool/ToolE, MTU-Bench) and program-synthesis/math reasoning benchmarks for dialogue.
claim_verdicts.verified_text: "Evaluating LLM-based Agents for Multi-Turn Conversations: A Survey" argues that multi-turn dialogues necessitate evaluating at the dialogue level rather than individual exchanges, prioritizing end-to-end experience because this holistic approach captures real-world performance. Complementing this, it devotes explicit attention to component-level evaluation of action and tool-use components, including multi-step tool selection and reasoning benchmarks such as MetaTool/ToolE, MTU-Bench, and program-synthesis and mathematical reasoning benchmarks tailored to dialogue contexts.

| Dimension | Evidence-backed comparison | |---|---| | Architecture | The paper "A Survey on Evaluation of LLM-based Agents" defines its scope as a comprehensive survey of evaluation methods for LLM-based agents, covering five perspectives: (1) core LLM capabilities for agentic workflows, (2) application-specific benchmarks such as web and SWE agents, (3) evaluation of generalist agents, (4) analysis of core benchmark dimensions, and (5) evaluation frameworks and tools for agent developers. It frames the field as a paradigm shift from static text-to-text LLMs to autonomous agents that plan, reason, and use tools in dynamic environments. [1][2][3][4]
The paper "Evaluating LLM-based Agents for Multi-Turn Conversations: A Survey" defines its scope as the evaluation of LLM-based agents specifically in multi-turn conversational settings, covering task-oriented and open-domain dialogue systems. It situates these systems historically from ELIZA through statistical and Transformer-based models to modern LLM-powered agents, and emphasizes the unique challenges of automated, reproducible, human-aligned evaluation for context-aware multi-turn interactions such as customer service and personal assistants. [5][6][7]
"A Survey on Evaluation of LLM-based Agents" analyzes agent benchmarks along shared orthogonal core dimensions: data curation, environment, interface, metric, and safety. Within safety and robustness, it highlights metrics such as pass^k (fraction of tasks successful across all k independent runs) and the need for "guardrail" metrics that penalize agents achieving task success via non-compliant actions like policy violations. The taxonomy is structural and cross-cutting rather than organized around what/how to evaluate. [1][8][9]
"Evaluating LLM-based Agents for Multi-Turn Conversations: A Survey" develops two interrelated taxonomy systems. The "what to evaluate" taxonomy identifies key components: task completion, response quality, user experience, memory and context retention, and planning and tool integration. The "how to evaluate" taxonomy categorizes methodologies into annotation-based evaluations, automated metrics (including BLEU, ROUGE, and BERTScore), hybrid strategies combining human and quantitative assessments, and self-judging methods utilizing LLMs. [10][11]
"A Survey on Evaluation of LLM-based Agents" surveys benchmarks across multiple categories: fundamental capability benchmarks (planning, tool use, self-reflection, memory); application-specific benchmarks for web agents, software engineering agents, scientific agents, and conversational agents; and generalist agent benchmarks such as Gaia, Gaia2, AgentBench, and the HAL (Holistic Agent Leaderboard). The survey observes a shift from application-specific toward general-purpose agents that integrate planning, reasoning, tool use, web interaction, file handling, and code execution. [12][13][14][2][1]
"Evaluating LLM-based Agents for Multi-Turn Conversations: A Survey" surveys holistic multi-turn dialogue benchmarks organized into categories such as dialogue modeling (HUMOD, BotChat, AgentBench), and tool and action integration (API-Bank, MMDU, MT-Mind2Web, MTU-Bench, Factcheck-Bench). The survey emphasizes benchmarks that assess realism, domain knowledge, long-horizon reasoning, multi-turn tool usage, user intent understanding, and factual veracity in conversational contexts. [5][15] | | Directionality | "A Survey on Evaluation of LLM-based Agents" covers both end-to-end and component-level evaluation. It organizes the field by agent capabilities (planning, tool use, self-reflection, memory) and by application domains, and explicitly identifies as a future direction the need for "advancing granular evaluation" beyond coarse-grained end-to-end success metrics, calling for standardized, fine-grained, step-by-step assessment of agent trajectories such as tool selection and reasoning quality. [2][13][16]
"Evaluating LLM-based Agents for Multi-Turn Conversations: A Survey" argues that multi-turn dialogues necessitate evaluating at the dialogue level rather than individual exchanges, prioritizing end-to-end experience because this holistic approach captures real-world performance. Complementing this, it devotes explicit attention to component-level evaluation of action and tool-use components, including multi-step tool selection and reasoning benchmarks such as MetaTool/ToolE, MTU-Bench, and program-synthesis and mathematical reasoning benchmarks tailored to dialogue contexts. items.span_text: We begin by discussing the evaluation of fundamental LLM-based agent capabilities (�2). These include planning, tool use, self-reflection, and memory. We then review benchmarks and evaluation strategies for prominent types of agentic applications: web agents, software engineering agents, scientific agents, and conversational agents (�3). We continue to describe benchmarks and leaderboards for evaluating generalist agents (�4). Consequently, we define and analyze core dimensions of agent benchmarks (�5). �6 reviews current evaluation frameworks for agent developers. These frameworks integrate with the agent�s development environment, and support its evaluation throughout the entire development cycle. We conclude with a discussion (�7) of current trends and future research directions in agent evaluation. Figure 1 offers a visual summary of the survey�s structure.
items.title: A Survey on Evaluation of LLM-based Agents

items.span_text: LLM-based agents represent a paradigm shift in AI, enabling autonomous systems to plan, reason, and use tools while interacting with dynamic environments. This paper provides the first comprehensive survey of evaluation methods for these increasingly capable agents. We analyze the field of agent evaluation across five perspectives: (1) Core LLM capabilities needed for agentic workflows, like planning, and tool use; (2) Application-specific benchmarks such as web and SWE agents; (3) Evaluation of generalist agents; (4) Analysis of agent benchmarks� core dimensions; and (5) Evaluation frameworks and tools for agent developers. Our analysis reveals current trends, including a shift toward more realistic, challenging evaluations with continuously updated benchmarks. We also identify critical gaps that future research must address�particularly in assessing cost-efficiency, safety, and robustness, and in developing fine-grained, scalable evaluation methods <sup>1</sup>.
items.title: A Survey on Evaluation of LLM-based Agents

items.span_text: The second approach for generalist agent evaluation relies on unifying several task-specific benchmarks into one. AgentBench (Liu et al., 2023) introduces interactive environments spanning OS operations, databases, games, and household tasks�highlighting core skills like flexibility and tool-based problem solving. HAL (Holistic Agent Leaderboard; Stroebl et al., 2025) provides a unified platform for benchmarks across domains, including coding and web. Yet, this approach does not support the same agent harness evaluation across any benchmark environment. Harbor and Exgentic aim to solve that by providing frameworks with a unified protocol for general agent assessment (Harbor Framework Team, 2026; Bandel et al., 2026a). These represent the first steps toward holistic, standardized, cross-environment agent evaluation (Lacoste et al., 2026).
items.title: A Survey on Evaluation of LLM-based Agents

items.span_text: Inclusion and Exclusion Criteria We defined strict criteria to maintain the survey�s focus and quality. Papers were included if they introduced a novel benchmark, an evaluation framework, or a significant methodological contribution to the assessment of LLM-based agents. Conversely, we excluded works that: 1. Proposed new agent architectures without a distinct contribution to evaluation methodology. 2. Focused solely on traditional LLM evaluation (e.g., reasoning on static datasets), lacking dynamic, agentic, or interactive components. While acceptance to top-tier conferences was utilized as a primary indicator of quality, we also included high-impact preprints to ensure the survey reflects the most recent advancements in this fast-paced field.
items.title: A Survey on Evaluation of LLM-based Agents

items.span_text: Multi-turn conversational agents powered by large language models (LLMs) mark a significant leap in conversational AI [74], ofering complex, context-aware interactions across multiple turns�essential for applications like customer service and personal assistants [38, 235]. Evaluating these systems poses unique challenges: methods must be automated, reproducible, and aligned with human judgments to ensure real-world reliability [247].

items.section: Evaluating LLM-based Agents for Multi-Turn Conversations: A Survey
items.span_text: This survey examines evaluation methods for large language model (LLM)-based agents in multi-turn conversational settings. Using a PRISMA-inspired framework, we systematically reviewed nearly 250 scholarly sources, capturing the state of the art from various venues of publication, and establishing a solid foundation for our analysis. Our study ofers a structured approach by developing two interrelated taxonomy systems: one that defines what to evaluate and another that explains how to evaluate. The first taxonomy identifies key components of LLM-based agents for multi-turn conversations and their evaluation dimensions, including task completion, response quality, user experience, memory and context retention, as well as planning and tool integration. These components ensure that the performance of conversational agents is assessed in a holistic and meaningful manner. The second taxonomy system focuses on the evaluation methodologies. It categorizes approaches into annotation-based evaluations, automated metrics, hybrid strategies that combine human assessments with quantitative measures, and self-judging methods utilizing LLMs. This framework not only captures traditional metrics derived from language understanding, such as BLEU and ROUGE scores, but also incorporates advanced techniques that reflect the dynamic, interactive nature of multi-turn dialogues. Together, these frameworks summarize the current status quo, expose limitations in traditional practices, and provide a structured blueprint for improvement. Based on the summarization of existing studies, we identify several challenges and propose future directions, including the development of scalable, real-time evaluation pipelines, enhanced privacy-preserving mechanisms, and robust metrics that capture dynamic multi-turn interactions. Our contributions bridge historical insights with modern practices, paving the way for next-generation, reliably evaluated conversational AI systems and ofering a comprehensive guide for researchers and practitioners
items.title: Evaluating LLM-based Agents for Multi-Turn Conversations: A Survey

items.span_text: � Holistic Evaluation Benchmarks and Datasets: As LLMs advanced, the field shifted toward more comprehensive benchmarks that incorporate human judgments, advanced automatic metrics such as BERTScore [97, 258], and task-specific evaluations to reflect the increasingly complex, context-rich nature of multi-turn interactions [10, 46]. The complexity of multi-turn dialogues led to the creation of specialized datasets and benchmarks tha focus on realism, domain knowledge, and long-horizon reasoning: (1) Dialogue Modeling: HUMOD [145] for human-like dialogue modeling; BotChat [50] for open-ended chat scenarios; AgentBench [133] for agent-centric tasks. (2) Tool and Action Integration: API-Bank [114] and MMDU [135] assess real-world tool usage, while MT-Mind2Web [46], MTU-Bench [225], and Factcheck-Bench [228] provide structured evaluations of multi-turn reasoning, user intent, and factual veracity.
items.title: Evaluating LLM-based Agents for Multi-Turn Conversations: A Survey

items.section: 2.2 Evaluating The Action and Tool-use Components of LLM-based Agents for Multi-turn Conversation
items.span_text: 2.2.2 Multi-step Tool Selection and Reasoning . Multi-step tool use requires the agent�s "chain of thought" in action. I is not enough to select the right tool once; the agent must demonstrate contextual awareness to know when tools are needed, sequential logic to order them correctly across turns, and domain resilience to maintain this logic in specialized fields like math or coding. MetaTool [85] evaluates LLMs� tool awareness and decision-making using the ToolE dataset, which tests single- and multi-tool scenarios. It highlights challenges in tool selection, revealing gaps in LLM capabilities Wang et al. [225] introduced MTU-Bench to assess multi-tool usage and multi-step interactions across scenarios, including single- and multi-turn tasks, enhancing action execution in conversations. Nijkamp et al. [157] proposed a program synthesis benchmark focusing on breaking down complex programming tasks, while Wang et al. [226] developed a Python-based benchmark for evaluating tool-use in responses. Mathematical reasoning benchmarks were introduced by Kurtic et al. [102] and Sun et al. [207], focusing on dialogue contexts. Guo et al. [68] designed a benchmark for assessing multi-turn tool-use in Chinese societal applications, and Zhuang et al. [275] emphasized open-ended tool usage in question answering. Lastly, Huang et al. [81] proposed a comprehensive benchmark to evaluate the full tool-use process, including planning and creation in complex tasks. These studies suggest that a unified evaluation framework for multi-step tool selection and reasoning should assess invocation precision, operational eficiency, and error recovery. It should also evaluate tool awareness, planning capability, execution accuracy, and multi-turn coherence.
items.title: Evaluating LLM-based Agents for Multi-Turn Conversations: A Survey

items.span_text: The shift from static models to adaptive, interactive agents calls for a new paradigm for evaluating LLM-based agents. Such evaluation must go beyond measuring LLM textual outputs to assess an agent�s capacity for sequential decision-making and operation within dynamic environments. It requires benchmarks that can assess the agent�s ability to accomplish user tasks via a sequence of actions and interactions. Moreover, benchmarks must co-evolve with agent capabilities, accommodating new classes of tasks and domains.
items.title: A Survey on Evaluation of LLM-based Agents

items.span_text: Safety and Robustness Metrics While most benchmarks prioritize capability, safety, and robustness are critical for enterprise adoption. Robustness is often quantified via pass^k, the fraction of tasks where the agent succeeds across all k independent runs. Beyond robustness, enterprise agents must adhere to strict policies, such as data privacy and access control, which are rarely tested in standard benchmarks. For instance, SWE-Lancer does not inherently penalize risky behaviors unless they interfere with replicating the target behavior. Future benchmarks must integrate "guardrail" metrics, penalizing agents that achieve task success via non-compliant actions (e.g., deleting production databases).
items.title: A Survey on Evaluation of LLM-based Agents

items.span_text: Realistic and Challenging Evaluation. While early evaluations often employed simplified, static environments, there is a clear shift toward more realistic and complex benchmarks. Web agents evolved from basic simulations to dynamic, realworld settings such as WebArena. In software engineering, benchmarks like SWE-bench utilize real GitHub issues, moving beyond synthetic coding tasks. Furthermore, there is increasing interest in long-horizon tasks typically performed by highly trained human experts, pushing evaluation closer to real-world professional workflows. This change reflects the growing need to assess agents� ability to derive practical value in realistic settings, to stresstest their limitations, and guide their progress.
items.title: A Survey on Evaluation of LLM-based Agents

items.span_text: Similar to the evolution of LLMs from task-specific into general-purpose models, agents are also transitioning from application-specific toward generalpurpose agents (Bandel et al., 2026b). Such tasks require integrated capabilities, from planning, reasoning, and tool use to web interaction, file handling, code execution, and more. This shift has led to the development of two complementary approaches to generalist agent evaluation.
items.title: A Survey on Evaluation of LLM-based Agents

items.span_text: Historically, multi-turn dialogue dates back to ELIZA [232], developed in the 1960s, which maintained simple states through pattern matching rather than complex context modeling. Subsequent progress incorporated finite-state machines, frame-based approaches, and statistical methods, culminating in the application of hidden Markov models (HMMs) [178] for spoken language understanding. Deep neural networks and Transformer architectures [217] then revolutionized context modeling, leveraging larger datasets and advanced training methods (including supervised and reinforcement learning) to handle more complex dialogues. In nowadays, multi-turn conversational systems often build on large-scale pre-trained language models, or namely Large Language Models (LLMs) [160], enabling advanced reasoning, continuity, and context handling over extended interactions.
items.title: Evaluating LLM-based Agents for Multi-Turn Conversations: A Survey

items.span_text: This paper provides a comprehensive overview of evaluation methods for LLM-based agents in multi-turn conversations. By systematically examining the evaluation goals�such as dialogue coherence, efective tool use, and memory retention�and the diverse methodologies, including annotation-based assessments and automated metrics, the study ofers a detailed synthesis of the progression from rule-based systems to transformer-based agents. This work lays a solid foundation for future explorations in the domain of conversational AI by addressing both theoretical and practical aspects. The core contribution of this paper lies in developing a structured taxonomy that clarifies critical dimensions of multi-turn conversational performance. This framework not only defines what elements should be evaluated but also establishes the methodological steps necessary to assess these components reliably. By synthesizing insights from nearly 200 scholarly sources, the study bridges the gap between existing evaluation methods and the emerging complexities inherent in modern LLM-based agents.

items.span_text: LLMs have recently made remarkable progress, tackling a wide range of challenging tasks. Yet, LLMs are static, having fixed knowledge, and confined to text-to-text interaction. LLM-based agents address those gaps by building on LLMs as a backbone, integrating them into multi-step workflows and equipping them with external tools (Wang et al., 2024a). Hence, LLM agents can perform computations, retrieve up-to-date information, and interact with their environment. Crucially, they can autonomously plan, execute, and adapt complex strategies in real-world settings. This agency enables them to tackle problems once beyond the reach of AI, unlocking innovative applications across diverse domains.

items.span_text: Advancing Granular Evaluation. Many current benchmarks rely on coarse-grained, end-to-end success metrics that, while useful for gauging overall performance, fall short in diagnosing specific agent failures. This lack of granularity obscures insights into intermediate decision processes such as tool selection and reasoning quality. Addressing this limitation calls for the development of standardized, fine-grained evaluation metrics targeting the trajectory of an agent�s task execution. Future work should explore detailed, step-by-step assessments to provide richer feedback and guide targeted improvements.

More recent benchmarks like SciRiff (Wadden et al., 2024) broaden the scope to instructionfollowing across scientific domains.

field_values.8b9e2f64d565137ca85ae75029e77fff_scope: Comprehensive survey of LLM-based agent evaluation across five perspectives: core capabilities (planning, tool use), application-specific benchmarks (web, SWE, scientific, conversational), generalist agents, core benchmark dimensions, and developer evaluation frameworks

claim_verdicts.reason: Abstract and introduction explicitly enumerate the five perspectives, the paradigm shift framing, and planning/reasoning/tool use capabilities.
claim_verdicts.verified_text: The paper "A Survey on Evaluation of LLM-based Agents" defines its scope as a comprehensive survey of evaluation methods for LLM-based agents, covering five perspectives: (1) core LLM capabilities for agentic workflows, (2) application-specific benchmarks such as web and SWE agents, (3) evaluation of generalist agents, (4) analysis of core benchmark dimensions, and (5) evaluation frameworks and tools for agent developers. It frames the field as a paradigm shift from static text-to-text LLMs to autonomous agents that plan, reason, and use tools in dynamic environments.

field_values.726cfbbd5683cdfabbf4c24e2bf8f0b2_scope: Evaluation methods for LLM-based agents in multi-turn conversational settings, spanning task-oriented and open-domain dialogue systems, historical context from ELIZA to LLM-based agents, and emphasis on automated, reproducible, human-aligned evaluation

claim_verdicts.reason: Keywords confirm task-oriented/open-domain dialogue; introduction confirms historical trajectory and emphasis on automated, reproducible, human-aligned evaluation for customer service/personal assistants.
claim_verdicts.verified_text: The paper "Evaluating LLM-based Agents for Multi-Turn Conversations: A Survey" defines its scope as the evaluation of LLM-based agents specifically in multi-turn conversational settings, covering task-oriented and open-domain dialogue systems. It situates these systems historically from ELIZA through statistical and Transformer-based models to modern LLM-powered agents, and emphasizes the unique challenges of automated, reproducible, human-aligned evaluation for context-aware multi-turn interactions such as customer service and personal assistants.

field_values.8b9e2f64d565137ca85ae75029e77fff_taxonom: Orthogonal core benchmark dimensions: data curation, environment, interface, metric, and safety (including pass^k robustness and guardrail metrics penalizing non-compliant task success)

claim_verdicts.reason: Section 5 explicitly defines the five orthogonal dimensions, defines pass^k, and describes guardrail metrics penalizing non-compliant actions.
claim_verdicts.verified_text: "A Survey on Evaluation of LLM-based Agents" analyzes agent benchmarks along shared orthogonal core dimensions: data curation, environment, interface, metric, and safety. Within safety and robustness, it highlights metrics such as pass^k (fraction of tasks successful across all k independent runs) and the need for "guardrail" metrics that penalize agents achieving task success via non-compliant actions like policy violations. The taxonomy is structural and cross-cutting rather than organized around what/how to evaluate.

field_values.726cfbbd5683cdfabbf4c24e2bf8f0b2_taxonom: Two interrelated taxonomies: (what) task completion, response quality, user experience, memory/context retention, planning and tool integration; (how) annotation-based, automated metrics (BLEU/ROUGE/BERTScore), hybrid human+quantitative, and LLM self-judging methods

claim_verdicts.reason: Abstract and conclusion explicitly enumerate both taxonomies with the listed components and methods, including BLEU, ROUGE, BERTScore and self-judging LLM methods.
claim_verdicts.verified_text: "Evaluating LLM-based Agents for Multi-Turn Conversations: A Survey" develops two interrelated taxonomy systems. The "what to evaluate" taxonomy identifies key components: task completion, response quality, user experience, memory and context retention, and planning and tool integration. The "how to evaluate" taxonomy categorizes methodologies into annotation-based evaluations, automated metrics (including BLEU, ROUGE, and BERTScore), hybrid strategies combining human and quantitative assessments, and self-judging methods utilizing LLMs.

field_values.8b9e2f64d565137ca85ae75029e77fff_benchma: Categories include fundamental capability benchmarks (planning, tool use, self-reflection, memory); application-specific (web, SWE, scientific, conversational); generalist benchmarks (Gaia, Gaia2, AgentBench, HAL); with a trend from application-specific toward general-purpose agents

claim_verdicts.reason: Introduction and Section 4 list fundamental capabilities, application-specific domains, generalist benchmarks (Gaia, Gaia2, AgentBench, HAL), and the shift toward general-purpose agents with integrated capabilities.
claim_verdicts.verified_text: "A Survey on Evaluation of LLM-based Agents" surveys benchmarks across multiple categories: fundamental capability benchmarks (planning, tool use, self-reflection, memory); application-specific benchmarks for web agents, software engineering agents, scientific agents, and conversational agents; and generalist agent benchmarks such as Gaia, Gaia2, AgentBench, and the HAL (Holistic Agent Leaderboard). The survey observes a shift from application-specific toward general-purpose agents that integrate planning, reasoning, tool use, web interaction, file handling, and code execution.

field_values.726cfbbd5683cdfabbf4c24e2bf8f0b2_benchma: Categories include dialogue modeling benchmarks (HUMOD, BotChat, AgentBench) and tool/action integration benchmarks (API-Bank, MMDU, MT-Mind2Web, MTU-Bench, Factcheck-Bench), emphasizing realism, long-horizon reasoning, multi-turn tool usage, and factual veracity

claim_verdicts.reason: Section 4.1 explicitly lists the dialogue modeling and tool/action integration benchmarks with stated emphasis on realism, domain knowledge, long-horizon reasoning, multi-turn tool usage, user intent, and factual veracity.
claim_verdicts.verified_text: "Evaluating LLM-based Agents for Multi-Turn Conversations: A Survey" surveys holistic multi-turn dialogue benchmarks organized into categories such as dialogue modeling (HUMOD, BotChat, AgentBench), and tool and action integration (API-Bank, MMDU, MT-Mind2Web, MTU-Bench, Factcheck-Bench). The survey emphasizes benchmarks that assess realism, domain knowledge, long-horizon reasoning, multi-turn tool usage, user intent understanding, and factual veracity in conversational contexts.

field_values.8b9e2f64d565137ca85ae75029e77fff_evaluat: Both end-to-end benchmarks (e.g., SWE-bench, WebArena) and component-level evaluation by capability (planning, tool use, self-reflection, memory); identifies a gap toward more granular, trajectory-level metrics as a future direction

claim_verdicts.reason: Introduction organizes by capabilities (planning, tool use, self-reflection, memory) and application domains; Section 7.2 explicitly calls for advancing granular evaluation beyond coarse-grained end-to-end success metrics with fine-grained trajectory assessment.
claim_verdicts.verified_text: "A Survey on Evaluation of LLM-based Agents" covers both end-to-end and component-level evaluation. It organizes the field by agent capabilities (planning, tool use, self-reflection, memory) and by application domains, and explicitly identifies as a future direction the need for "advancing granular evaluation" beyond coarse-grained end-to-end success metrics, calling for standardized, fine-grained, step-by-step assessment of agent trajectories such as tool selection and reasoning quality.

field_values.726cfbbd5683cdfabbf4c24e2bf8f0b2_evaluat: Prioritizes end-to-end dialogue-level evaluation that captures real-world multi-turn performance, while also providing component-level evaluation of action and tool-use (e.g., MetaTool/ToolE, MTU-Bench, multi-step reasoning and math benchmarks in dialogue contexts)

claim_verdicts.reason: Section 2.1 explicitly prioritizes end-to-end dialogue-level evaluation; Section 2.2 explicitly covers multi-step tool selection (MetaTool/ToolE, MTU-Bench) and program-synthesis/math reasoning benchmarks for dialogue.
claim_verdicts.verified_text: "Evaluating LLM-based Agents for Multi-Turn Conversations: A Survey" argues that multi-turn dialogues necessitate evaluating at the dialogue level rather than individual exchanges, prioritizing end-to-end experience because this holistic approach captures real-world performance. Complementing this, it devotes explicit attention to component-level evaluation of action and tool-use components, including multi-step tool selection and reasoning benchmarks such as MetaTool/ToolE, MTU-Bench, and program-synthesis and mathematical reasoning benchmarks tailored to dialogue contexts.
